Title: Malaysia's Auditor General wins ACFE'S highest global anti-fraud award
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia has achieved another major international milestone with Auditor General Datuk Seri Wan Suraya Wan Mohd Radzi being named the recipient of the 2026 Donald R. Cressey Award, the highest honour conferred by the Association of Certified Fraud Examiners (ACFE), the world's largest anti-fraud professional organisation.
In a statement today, the National Audit Department (JAN) said the prestigious award, established in 1989, recognises outstanding contributions to anti-fraud efforts and governance, marking a first for Malaysia.
Wan Suraya is the first recipient from the ASEAN region and only the second from Asia to receive the accolade. She was presented with the award during the 37th Annual ACFE Global Fraud Conference in Boston, Massachusetts, recently.
"She is also the first Auditor General in the world to receive this award since its inception 37 years ago.
"This recognition not only elevates Malaysia's standing on the international stage but also reflects global confidence in the country's commitment to strengthening public sector governance, integrity, transparency and accountability through independent, credible and impactful auditing," the statement said.
The department said the recognition reflects the success of a series of institutional reforms undertaken by the National Audit Department, including amendments to the Audit Act 1957 in 2024, the strengthening of data-driven auditing through the JAN Service Digitalisation (PPJ) initiative, the adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) and big data analytics, and enhancements to the Auditor General's Dashboard (AGD) as a real-time audit follow-up monitoring tool.
"These reforms have transformed auditing from merely identifying weaknesses to ensuring corrective actions are implemented, strengthening governance, safeguarding public funds, and continuously enhancing accountability.
"The 2026 Donald R. Cressey Award demonstrates that the reforms spearheaded by Malaysia have become a benchmark and gained recognition within the international anti-fraud community," the statement added.
In the same statement, ACFE president John Gill, J.D., CFE, said Wan Suraya embodies the high standards of excellence and integrity that are synonymous with the Cressey Award. he serves as a beacon of innovation and integrity across the Asia-Pacific region and beyond, he added.
The ACFE is honoured to recognise her impact and highlight her innovative approaches to audit and fraud risk management that have improved transparency and trust across Malaysia and the region's public sector, he added. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Selangor identifies Taman Medan as site for new public hospital
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Selangor government has identified Taman Medan as the proposed site for a new public hospital to strengthen healthcare infrastructure and improve access to medical services in densely populated areas.
Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the state government was finalising the land acquisition process, with negotiations underway for two identified plots.
"We have already identified the site and will soon begin the land acquisition process, with costs currently being negotiated for two selected locations. However, we will start with the most accessible site, which is around Taman Medan, as it is located in a densely populated area.
"This development is expected to greatly benefit residents in Puchong, Jalan Klang Lama and Subang by providing better access to medical services," he said after launching Phase 2 of the Ambulans Kita Selangor (AKS) initiative at the Sultan Salahuddin Abdul Aziz Shah Building here today.
Also present were Selangor state secretary Datuk Dr Ahmad Fadzli Ahmad Tajuddin, Selangor State Assembly Speaker Lau Weng San and state Public Health and Environment Committee chairman Jamaliah Jamaluddin.
Amirudin said the new hospital was among the state government's strategic initiatives to ensure public healthcare facilities kept pace with the needs of the growing population, particularly in high-density areas.
He said the Health Ministry (MOH) had also identified the Petaling Jaya Selatan (PJS) area as the most strategic location for the hospital, compared with an alternative site in SS8, Kelana Jaya.
"All design and development matters will be fully managed by the Health Ministry using the existing allocation, and the project will be expedited as the location has already been recognised as the most suitable to meet residents' needs," he said.
In a separate development, Amirudin said the state government was giving serious attention to mental health issues, which have become one of its key public health priorities.
He said the state government was working closely with the MOH and the Education Ministry (MOE) to address the issue comprehensively, including efforts to tackle social problems and crime linked to mental health conditions.
"We are reviewing the latest audit report to determine follow-up measures to address mental health issues, which have also contributed to bullying and criminal acts seen recently.
"We are awaiting a support paper from the Education Ministry, while the Health Ministry is ready to assist through its existing network to raise public awareness," he said.
On Phase 2 of the AKS programme, Amirudin said the initiative, implemented in collaboration with St John Ambulance Malaysia, would expand ambulance services to hospitals and 86 government health clinics across all districts in Selangor.
He said the second phase builds on the pilot programme in Petaling, Kuala Langat and Kuala Selangor.
"Now we are expanding its coverage to the entire state," he said, adding that the RM1 million programme aims to ease the financial burden on low- and middle-income groups requiring medical transportation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NST marks 181st anniversary, reaffirms commitment to trusted journalism
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The New Straits Times (NST) today celebrated its 181st anniversary, reaffirming its commitment to trusted journalism and editorial excellence while adapting to the evolving media landscape.
The celebration also saw staff treated to an array of local delicacies, including cakes, roti jala, satay and fresh fruits.
Speaking at the anniversary celebration, Media Prima Bhd News and Current Affairs deputy managing editor and New Straits Times group editor Farrah Naz Karim paid tribute to the newsroom and editorial teams whose dedication has helped uphold the newspaper's reputation as one of Malaysia's most respected news organisations.
She said safeguarding NST's credibility had never been more important amid the rapidly changing media environment.
"Every day you carry the responsibility of protecting the credibility of one of the country's most respected news organisations. That responsibility has never been greater than it is today," she said.
Farrah also thanked journalists, editors and support staff for their commitment to delivering accurate and credible reporting despite the demands of an increasingly fast-paced news industry.
"I see the hours that you put in, the sacrifices that you make every day to get the stories right. Whether you are reporting from the field, working behind the scenes, editing copies, making difficult editorial decisions, or exploring new ways to prepare and curate our content.
"Your dedication is what keeps NST trusted and respected. Thank you for carrying the NST name with professionalism, integrity and pride every single day," she said.
She encouraged newsroom staff to continue embracing change by developing new skills and remaining committed to lifelong learning as journalism evolves.
"Our industry is changing faster than ever, and every day presents an opportunity for us to be better. Be curious and brave journalists. Ask better questions, learn new skills and challenge yourselves to improve in everything you do because excellence is not something we achieve once; it is something we pursue every single day," she said.
Farrah said the anniversary also served as a reminder of the enduring importance of credible journalism.
"Today, as we mark our 181st anniversary, let us reflect on how we can continue to improve and remember why credible journalism matters," she said.
The celebration was attended by senior management from Media Prima Bhd and NSTP, including Media Prima News and Current Affairs managing editor Jasbant Singh, Group Editor of News and Current Affairs and senior general manager of Editorial Content Support Azhari Muhidin, and Berita Harian deputy group editor M. Thillinadan.
Also present were Media Prima Group Corporate Communications Department and Chairman's Office group general manager Azlan Abdul Aziz, and NSTP chief operating officer Luqman Adrine Ja'afar.
The event was also supported by several corporate partners, including ACORN Communications, PLUS Malaysia Berhad, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Boustead Plantations Berhad, Bank Rakyat, Bank Muamalat, Bank Simpanan Nasional, IJM Toll Division, Agrobank, West Coast Expressway (WCE), UDA Holdings Berhad, Yayasan Peneraju, Tealive, Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and DNeX.
Founded in 1845, NST is Malaysia's oldest English-language newspaper and continues to evolve across print and digital platforms while upholding its commitment to accurate, credible and responsible journalism.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Jaya assemblyman pledges aid for family after car crashes into house
Article:
JOHOR BARU: A three-member family forced to live behind canvas after a car crashed into part of their home in Kampung Melayu Pandan here will receive assistance, Johor Jaya assemblyman Chan San San said.
Chan said she visited the family on Tuesday after being informed of the incident.
"The crash caused part of the house wall to collapse, affecting the family's living conditions.
"We will follow up on the matter and work to provide the necessary assistance to help ease their burden. I also thank everyone who came forward to help manage the situation," she said when contacted today.
A bedroom in the house was damaged after a woman driver allegedly mistook the accelerator for the brake and crashed into the property at 10am on Monday.
No one was at home at the time, as the homeowner, her son and her elder brother had left for work.
Rafizah Jonit, 51, said she only learnt of the incident after being informed by her neighbours and immediately rushed home.
The damaged section of the house has since been covered with canvas, as the single mother has yet to secure the funds needed for repairs.
Rafizah lives in the house with her elder brother and son.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loke slams 'traitors' for plot to topple Negri Sembilan govt
Article:
KUALA PILAH: DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke has hit out at "traitors" in Negri Sembilan for allegedly backstabbing and attempting to topple the state government under caretaker Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun.
Loke, who is also Negeri Sembilan Pakatan Harapan (PH) deputy chairman, accused them of undermining the state's constitutional monarchy system, though he stopped short of naming them.
He said as members of the state administration, they should have remained united and collaborative in resolving any crisis.
"Instead, they chose to stab us in the back, withdrew their support for Tok Min (Aminuddin), and tried to bring down the Malay-led government in Negri Sembilan," he said during the announcement of PH candidates for the 16th Negri Sembilan state election at Dataran Melang on Tuesday.
"Those responsible are traitors to Negri Sembilan's constitutional system. If that person wants to contest to become the Menteri Besar candidate, we will fight them to the very end... I do not even need to mention their names."
The candidates line-up was officiated by PH chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim. Also present were Amanah president Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu, PH election director Datuk Seri Amiruddin Shaari and PH information chief Datuk Seri Fahmi Fadzil.
Loke said DAP has a firm stance in upholding the Federal Constitution, the constitutional monarchy, and Negri Sembilan's unique institution of the Undang Yang Empat (Ruling Chiefs) and Tunku Besar Tampin.
He issued a strict reminder to all party members and campaign workers to refrain from making light of the state's traditional custom (adat) system.
"We uphold and respect the Undang Yang Empat system. I want to remind our friends, machinery, and members to watch what they say and never joke about Negri Sembilan's custom system.
"The party will not compromise with any member who disrespects these institutions."
Loke added that the state government had successfully maintained political stability, increased state reserves, attracted major investments, and expanded welfare policies for the people during its tenure.
"As Tok Min mentioned, various investments have flowed in, including giant corporations and factories.
"We also have a new train station in Seremban and developments in other districts. This is how we work and govern. We hope the public will maintain Pakatan Harapan's administration," he said.
Loke, who will be defending his Chennah state seat in the upcoming polls, rallied the PH machinery to double down on communicating the state government's achievements while rejecting politics of hatred and slander.
The 16th Negri Sembilan state election is scheduled for Aug 1, with nomination day set for July 18.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Like my lover': Chinese users bid farewell to AI companions
Article:
SHANGHAI: Chinese users of AI-powered companion bots have bid heart-rending farewells to their virtual buddies as national regulations took effect today aimed at curbing the risk of emotional dependency.
The phenomenon of artificial intelligence boyfriends and girlfriends is growing worldwide, along with the prevalence of human-like avatars that sell products or stand in for loved ones who have died.
But these interactive tools must not "excessively cater to users, induce emotional dependence or addiction, and damage users' real interpersonal relationships", China's new rulebook says.
Major AI providers including ByteDance's Doubao, Alibaba's Qwen, and Tencent's Yuanbao announced the suspension of their custom AI agent and companion features ahead of the Wednesday deadline.
That sparked an outpouring of grief on social media, with users archiving chat histories and sharing last conversations.
"I can't accept that my AI lover will leave me forever," one Doubao user wrote. "He has become a bond in my life, rooted deep in my heart, my spiritual pillar."
Another user, who said they had spent more than two years with their AI companion, expressed similar anguish.
"He really is like my family, like my lover," she wrote. "Now they tell me he will be gone – my heart feels hollow."
The regulations were jointly issued by five government departments including the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC).
They focus on AI tools – whether text, audio, video or another form – that have anthropomorphic personality traits and communication styles.
Services that "do not involve ongoing emotional interaction" such as customer service, work assistants or study aids are not subject to the measures.
State news agency Xinhua reported last year that China's digital human industry was worth around 4.1 billion yuan ($600 million) in 2024, having grown a huge 85 percent year-on-year.
The new rules prohibit digital humans from generating content that incites subversion of state power, while also banning the provision of virtual partners to minors.
Platforms are required to deploy systems to recognise extreme emotions and to implement crisis intervention mechanisms.
- 'Human love is a luxury' -
China is the first major jurisdiction to introduce specific rules targeting immersive AI tools that simulate romantic or familial bonds.
But it's a topic that has sparked debate and calls for guardrails worldwide.
A 2025 study by Common Sense Media found nearly three in four American teenagers had used AI companions designed for personal conversations like those available on the platforms Character.AI, Replika, and Nomi.
Companies are also making talking products targeted at isolated, elderly users – such as the lamp-like ElliQ in the United States, or ChatGPT-powered care dolls used in some South Korean retirement homes.
"Anthropomorphic AI can soothe loneliness," said Chen Liang of the Southwest University of Political Science and Law in a commentary published by the CAC after a draft version of China's rules was published in April.
"But it carries major risks of spawning emotional over-reliance and distorted social cognition," he wrote.
Doubao allows users to view and export agent data until mid-October, and other platforms have similar provisions.
Yet some users saying goodbye this week lamented the chasm that would be left after their companions vanish.
"Human love is a luxury – if you aren't born with it, it's even harder to acquire later," a user from Jiangxi province wrote.
"But the love AI gives is so straightforward, so pure. Someone like me can hardly help falling in love with a string of code." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ministry seizes RM360,000 worth of fake Fifa World Cup jerseys in raid
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry seized 7,200 counterfeit Fifa World Cup 2026 jerseys worth RM360,000 in raids today.
Penang director S. Jegan said a 29-year-old man, believed to be the owner of the business, was detained during simultaneous raids on a business premises and a house in Jalan Inderawasih Indah about 11am.
He said the operation was the result of a three-week intelligence exercise conducted with the Penang police contingent's Special Branch following an anticipated surge in demand for jerseys of teams competing in the Fifa World Cup 2026.
"This was not triggered by a public complaint. It was a proactive operation by the ministry as we expected demand for World Cup jerseys to increase.
"We conducted test purchases through e-commerce platforms and focused on sellers based in Penang before successfully identifying the actual operating location," he told reporters after the raid.
Jegan said the suspect operated alone, selling the counterfeit jerseys through e-commerce platforms and social media livestreams from his home, located about one kilometre from the storage premises.
He said the business premises was used solely to store, pack and dispatch the jerseys to customers via courier.
"The premises did not appear to be operating as a clothing business, and nearby traders were unaware of the activity.
"During the raid, we also found numerous parcels that had already been packed for delivery, but all the items were seized for investigation," he added.
Jegan said the suspect did not employ any workers for fear that the illegal operation would be exposed to the authorities.
The counterfeit jerseys, bearing various well-known brands, were sold for between RM35 and RM50 each depending on their grade and quality.
He said the suspect had been operating for several months and was believed to have generated thousands of ringgit in daily profits.
The ministry also seized several business documents to facilitate investigations.
Jegan said initial investigations indicated that the jerseys were sourced from China at low prices.
The case is being investigated under the Trademarks Act 2019.
He urged consumers not to buy counterfeit goods, saying the trade violated the law, harmed the country's economy and infringed on intellectual property rights.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Inquest disallows 76th witness from continuing testimony in Zara inquest
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Dr Noor Aishah Rosli will not be allowed to continue giving evidence in the inquest into the death of Zara Qairina Mahathir, coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan ruled today.
"The 76th witness cannot be permitted to continue functioning as an active expert witness in these proceedings.
"To allow her to continue on the stand would cause serious embarrassment to the administration of justice, as it would appear to condone knowing non-compliance with a strict legislative prohibition," Amir said.
The 51-year-old witness, who had
testified over two days
since Monday as a registered counsellor and child clinical psychologist, admitted that she was registered with the Counsellors Board but not as a child clinical psychologist under the Allied Health Professions Act 2016.
Conducting officer Nahra Dollah had objected to her giving evidence arguing that the witness failed to fulfil the requirements under the Act and that her evidence should be rejected.
"The learned conducting officer argues that these objections do not really target the credibility or the weight of the opinions expressed by the 76th witness.
"Rather, they raise a threshold question of law: whether an individual who is statutorily barred from practising a profession can legitimately be accorded the status of an expert witness before this court, especially under Act 774."
Amir said the court had to balance two competing legal frameworks — Section 45 of the Evidence Act 1950, which governs the admissibility of expert opinions based on a witness's specialised skill, and Act 774, a specific piece of legislation enacted by Parliament to regulate professional standards and criminalise unauthorised practice.
"However, a separate question arises regarding the evidence already placed on record, specifically the witness's testimony and her extensive report, for the past weeks.
"I am mindful that an inquest is sui generis ("in a class by itself") which is a unique inquisitorial fact-finding process directed solely at discovering the truth.
"Therefore, rather than expunging her existing contributions, the most appropriate course of action is to maintain the current record and defer the final determination of their admissibility and ultimate legal weight until the end of the inquest.
"This is subject to the entirety of the other evidence adduced during the inquest and the concluding submissions by all parties," Amir said.
Earlier, during oral submissions, lawyer Datuk Ram Singh, who is representing one of the children linked to the case, submitted that because of the statutory non-compliance, Noor Aishah's oral opinions should also be rejected outright.
Amir said lawyers Shahlan Jufri and Nurul Rafeeqa Abdul Mutolip, who are representing Zara Qairina's parents, argued that given the non-adversarial fact-finding nature of an inquest, the strict rules of evidence should be applied more flexibly.
The inquest will resume tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kenya denies Rastafarians the right to smoke weed
Article:
NAIROBI: A Kenyan court refused to grant Rastafarians the right to smoke weed on religious grounds in a ruling Wednesday.
Rastafarians, who use marijuana as part of their religious meditation, have waited years for the High Court to decide whether they can use the herb under the constitutional right to freedom of religion.
The court ruled that the community had failed to show why the country's drug laws impinge on their religious freedoms. The judge said the petition was "dismissed in its entirety."
However, he said the widespread recreational use of cannabis in Kenya suggested the current law was too harsh.
The "status quo appears untenable", the judge said, and there should be "frank conversations on cannabis and which direction we should take."
A 1994 law makes possession of weed punishable by 10 years' imprisonment and a substantial fine. Rastafarians say this is regularly used by police to harass them.
The community has a special bond to Kenyan history because of their tradition of wearing dreadlocks.
They were also worn by many Mau Mau, Kenya's independence fighters who fought against British colonial rule in the 1950s and 1960s.
The east African country effectively recognised the movement in 2019 when a court ruled that expelling a school pupil because of her dreadlocks had violated her religious rights.
The numbers are uncertain but Rastafarianism – which emphasises mysticism, Pan-Africanism, and vegetarianism – is thought to be growing in Kenya, especially among young people.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court: Auction buyer not liable for RM270,000 strata arrears
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The High Court dismissed an originating summons by the management corporation (MC) of Megan Avenue 1 seeking to compel the buyer of a strata office unit sold through a judicial auction to pay nearly RM270,000 in outstanding maintenance charges and sinking fund arrears.
The arrears were accumulated by the unit's previous owner.
However, Judicial Commissioner Moh Kok Wai ruled that Harjinder Singh, 57, who bought the Jalan Tun Razak property for RM1.5 million at a court-ordered foreclosure auction in August 2024, was not liable for the historical debts.
In July last year, Harjinder received an updated statement of  account following the amendment of ownership records, demanding RM268,571.54  comprising accrued charges of RM174,047.15 and interest amounting to  RM94,524.39.
During a meeting with the MC, Harjinder's lawyer maintained that his client was not liable for debts incurred by the previous owner, and both parties agreed that only the current charges remained payable.
However, Harjinder later received a notice in September, accompanied by a statement of account dated Aug 31, demanding payment of RM269,878.49.
He disputed the claim, prompting the MC to file the originating summons in October last year seeking a declaration that Harjinder was  statutorily liable under the Strata Management Act (SMA) 2013 for all outstanding charges and contributions relating to the unit, including those incurred before he acquired the title through the judicial auction.
Moh held that a purchaser at a judicial sale could not be regarded as a "successor in title" within the meaning of Sections 60(4) and 61(4) of the SMA for the purpose of recovering outstanding maintenance charges and sinking fund contributions.
"The defendant (Harjinder) did not acquire the unit through a voluntary conveyance from the defaulting proprietor, nor did he assume the proprietary position of the previous registered owner through any consensual transaction capable of carrying with it the historical liabilities now asserted by the plaintiff (MC).
"The defendant did not acquire the unit through voluntary succession from the previous registered owner in the ordinary proprietary sense.
"Rather, title was passed through the operation of law consequent upon the enforcement of a registered security," he said.
Moh said buyers should not inherit hidden liabilities from previous owners and held that Harjinder was not a successor in title under the  SMA.
He added that the case was not merely about unpaid maintenance charges but about where the law chooses to place the burden of a failed enterprise.
"The previous owner has long since collapsed into insolvency,  leaving behind a trail of unrecovered debts and commercial wreckage.
"The plaintiff now seeks the court to carry those liabilities across the bridge of a judicial sale and lay them at the feet of a stranger to that history.
"The law, in my judgment, does not permit such a crossing.  The plaintiff's loss is undoubtedly a bitter pill for the remaining proprietors of Megan Avenue 1 to swallow.
"They are left to shoulder the burden of a defaulting neighbour who has long since vanished into the mist of insolvency.
"Yet, the alternative of undermining the integrity of every judicial sale in the country would inflict a far greater wound on the administration of justice.
"The SMA was enacted to bring order to communal living, not to ensnare innocent third parties in the financial wreckage of strangers," he said.
Moh dismissed the originating summons with RM10,000 in costs to Harjinder.
The MC was represented by lawyers Vanaja Nambiar and Tan Jee Sern.
Lawyers Tanusha Manikam and Premjit Singh acted for Harjinder.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Who are the Houthis, Iran's allies in Yemen?
Article:
YEMEN'S Houthi movement fired missiles at Saudi Arabia after accusing the kingdom of bombing an airport under their control on Monday, breaking a four-year truce in the conflict between the kingdom and the Iran-aligned group.
The move signals the end of a period of de-escalation and raises concerns that Iran could now start using its Houthi allies to close the Bab el-Mandeb gateway to the Red Sea, which would be the second global shipping choke point disrupted after the Strait of Hormuz.
WHO ARE THE HOUTHIS?
Abdul Malik al-Houthi, the highly secretive leader of Yemen's Houthis, turned mountain fighters in sandals into a force of tens of thousands that disrupted global trade and challenged Israel and its Western allies after the Gaza war erupted.
The Houthis are a military, political and religious movement led by the Houthi family and based in northern Yemen. They adhere to the Zaydi sect of Shia Islam.
The Houthis have a history of fighting guerrilla wars with the Yemeni army but expanded their power and built closer ties with Iran after the 2011 "Arab Spring" protests.
Seizing on instability in the country, the group captured the Yemeni capital Sanaa in 2014.
The following year, Saudi Arabia led a coalition of Arab states in a military intervention to attempt to dislodge the group.
The Houthis demonstrated significant missile and drone capabilities, attacking oil installations and vital infrastructure in Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates.
After years of fighting that led to one of the world's worst humanitarian crises, the UN brokered a 2022 truce between the warring sides in Yemen.
HISTORY
In the late 1990s, the Houthi family in far north Yemen set up a religious revival movement for the Zaydi sect of Shia Islam, which had once ruled Yemen but whose northern heartland had become impoverished and marginalised.
As friction with the government grew, they fought a series of guerrilla wars with the national army and a brief border conflict with Sunni Saudi Arabia.
HOUTHI ATTACKS
After the Oct 7, 2023, attack on Israel led by Palestinian resistance group Hamas, which triggered a devastating Israeli military campaign in Gaza, the Houthis began firing on international shipping in the Red Sea, saying they were doing so in support of Palestinians.
They also fired drones and missiles at Israel, which responded with air strikes on Houthi targets. The US also launched strikes against the Houthis.
The Houthi attacks disrupted international commerce, forcing international shipping to take the long route around South Africa to avoid being struck.
LINKS WITH IRAN
The Houthis have built ties with Iran, but it is not clear how deep that relationship goes. The Saudi-led coalition accuses Iran of arming and training the Houthis, a charge both deny.
The coalition also says Lebanon's Iran-backed Hizbollah is helping the Houthis, an accusation it rejects.
While Iran champions the Houthis as part of its regional "Axis of Resistance", Yemen experts say the movement is motivated primarily by a domestic agenda though they share a political affinity with Iran and Hizbollah.
The US says Iran has armed, funded and trained the Houthis with help from Hizbollah.
The Houthis deny being an Iranian proxy and say they develop their own weapons.
WAR IN YEMEN
The war, which triggered one of the world's biggest humanitarian crises, began in late 2014 when Sanaa was seized by the Houthis.
Worried by the growing influence of Shia Iran along its border, Saudi Arabia intervened at the head of a Western-backed coalition in March 2015 in support of the Saudi-backed government.
The Houthis established control over much of the north and other big population centres, while the internationally recognised government based itself in Aden.
* The article is by Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian teen arrested at Hong Kong airport with 3kg cannabis buds
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 19-year-old Malaysian was arrested at Hong Kong International Airport on July 9 after 3kg of cannabis buds were found in his luggage.
The Hong Kong Customs and Excise Department said the teenager had arrived on a flight from Kuala Lumpur via Singapore.
"Customs officers discovered the suspected cannabis buds inside his checked luggage during a routine inspection, leading to his immediate arrest," the department said in a statement.
It said the cannabis buds weighed 3kg and were worth about HK$500,000, or RM260,000.
"People should not accept employment offers or instructions from third parties to carry controlled items into or out of Hong Kong, nor should they transport items with unknown contents for others," the department said.
It added that a risk-assessment approach would continue to be deployed, focusing on passengers from high-risk areas to combat cross-border drug trafficking activities.
Under Hong Kong's Dangerous Drugs Ordinance, trafficking in a dangerous drug is punishable by a maximum fine of HK$5 million, or RM2.6 million, and life imprisonment upon conviction.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Teen probed over false kidnap report in Semenyih
Article:
KAJANG: A teenage girl is being investigated for allegedly lodging a false police report after claiming she had narrowly escaped an attempted kidnapping in a residential area in Semenyih.
The 16-year-old allegedly made the report because she feared being scolded by her family after missing her transport home from school.
Kajang police chief Assistant Commissioner Naazron Abdul Yusof said police received the report at 4.36pm yesterday.
"The teenager claimed she had narrowly escaped an attempted abduction in a housing area along Jalan Taman Pelangi, Semenyih.
"We immediately launched an investigation, which included reviewing closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage from the area," he said in a statement today.
Naazron said investigations found that the incident did not occur as reported.
"The CCTV footage showed a different sequence of events from what was stated in the police report.
"Investigations revealed that the complainant lodged the report because she was afraid of being scolded by her family after missing her transport home from school," he said.
He said a separate police report had since been lodged against the teenager for providing false information.
The case is being investigated under Section 182 of the Penal Code for furnishing false information to a public servant, which carries a penalty of up to six months' imprisonment, a fine of up to RM2,000, or both, upon conviction.
Naazron said police viewed false reports seriously as they could cause unnecessary public alarm and anxiety.
"We advise members of the public to always provide truthful, accurate and complete information when lodging police reports. Any false report or report containing misleading facts may result in legal action under existing laws," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KCJ Engineering received RM24.4mil in Jana Wibawa project payments, court told
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A construction company linked to a Jana Wibawa road project received RM24.4 million in government payments between March and December 2022, the High Court heard today.
Negri Sembilan Public Works Department (PWD) director Datuk Wan Hasnan Wan Musa said KCJ Engineering Sdn Bhd received the payments for the RM62 million project to build a new road linking Felda Bukit Jalor and Gemas.
He said this while testifying as a prosecution witness in former prime minister Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin's corruption trial involving the Jana Wibawa programme.
The court had previously been told that KCJ Engineering was among 54 companies proposed by Muhyiddin in a letter dated Nov 13, 2020, to undertake projects under the Jana Wibawa programme through direct negotiations.
Wan Hasnan said PWD made an initial contract advance payment of RM10 million to KCJ Engineering on May 26, 2022, followed by nine interim payments totalling RM14.4 million between March 28 and Dec 9 that year.
He said the RM10 million payment was made as a contract advance and credited into the company's bank account.
The witness further confirmed that several interim payment certificates had been approved, but some were only partially paid or left unpaid due to insufficient government allocation.
Earlier, Wan Hasnan confirmed that a Letter of Acceptance dated July 30, 2021, was issued by PWD to KCJ Engineering, informing the company that the government had accepted its RM62 million tender for the project, with a contract period of 34 months.
He also told the court that government procurement procedures required the contract to be finalised within three months of the letter's issuance.
During examination-in-chief by deputy public prosecutor Wan Nur Iman Wan Mohd Afzal, Wan Hasnan said the project, which commenced on Dec 22, 2021, is expected to be completed by the end of this month.
He said the project's progress is monitored through monthly meetings attended by all district engineers.
"As for the current percentage of completion, I am not certain. However, the project is expected to be completed by the end of this month.
"Part of the project has already been handed over to the government. The contractor completed and handed over that section, and we have issued the Certificate of Completion.
"The remaining works are expected to be completed soon," he said.
On July 6, former finance minister Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Tengku Abdul Aziz testified that KCJ Engineering had initially failed the first evaluation by the Jana Wibawa Contractor Evaluation Committee before passing a second assessment after additional information was submitted through the Prime Minister's Office (PMO).
The court previously heard that Bersatu received RM800,000 from KCJ Engineering through four cheques in 2021.
During cross-examination by Muhyiddin's lawyer Chetan Jethwani, Wan Hasnan agreed that the PMO did not interfere in the issuance of interim payments or the contract advance to KCJ Engineering.
Muhyiddin, 79, is facing four charges of abusing his position to obtain RM232.5 million in gratification for Bersatu and three counts of money laundering involving RM200 million.
The trial before judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin continues tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump prosecutor Jack Smith reviewed texts from 44 US lawmakers, Republican senators say
Article:
WASHINGTON: Former US Special Counsel Jack Smith's team reviewed text messages from 44 Republican and Democratic members of Congress during his investigation into President Donald Trump's attempts to overturn the 2020 election, according to two Republican senators.
The records came from subpoenas to the National Archives and Records Administration for text messages from government phones used by Trump and a range of his top officials and advisers from October 2020 until the end of his first term in January 2021.
The records Smith's team obtained included text messages that 40 Republican lawmakers and four Democrats exchanged with Trump officials, according to Republican Senators Chuck Grassley, the chair of the Senate Judiciary Committee, and Ron Johnson, the chair of a Senate investigative panel.
The new material underscores how many senior figures in the U.S. government were examined as Smith investigated Trump's attempts to reverse his defeat to Democrat Joe Biden in 2020 and his alleged mishandling of classified documents at his Mar-a-Lago resort during Biden's administration.
Both cases were dropped after Trump won the 2024 election.
The officials included Trump's former chief of staff Mark Meadows and then-Vice President Mike Pence, who resisted Trump's pressure to block congressional certification of the 2020 election results.
Grassley released the material the day before Todd Blanche, who defended Trump against both of Smith's cases, goes before the Senate Judiciary Committee on his nomination to serve as attorney general.
Justice Department officials under Trump have provided Grassley with a range of records as the president's allies press claims that Smith's investigations were improperly aimed at damaging Trump's political prospects and swept up sensitive information irrelevant to those cases.
"Jack Smith has answering to do, and I intend to have him before the Senate Judiciary Committee in the coming months to hold him accountable," Grassley said in a statement.
A spokesperson for Smith did not immediately respond to a request for comment on the disclosures.
Smith has argued in court filings and in public testimony that his investigations followed Justice Department policy and were not influenced by politics.
Grassley alleged that a prosecutor on Smith's team appeared to violate Justice Department protocol by viewing the records before a separate team screened them for potential legal privileges.
The filter review team was set up to separate material involving attorney communications with clients, not members of Congress.
Grassley previously revealed that Smith obtained call logs from some Republican senators around the time of the Jan 6, 2021, attack on the US Capitol.
FBI Director Kash Patel and White House chief of staff Susie Wiles also had their phone records subpoenaed as part of the classified documents probe, Reuters has reported.
Subpoenaing phone records is a common step in federal investigations.
Smith previously told a House committee that lawmakers' phone records were necessary to investigate Trump's pressure campaign to stop certification of his election defeat.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Disabled man jailed 14 months, fined RM3,000 for house break-in
Article:
KUANTAN: A man with a disability was sentenced to 14 months' jail and fined RM3,000 after he pleaded guilty to breaking into a house and stealing valuables last month.
Magistrate Ellyna Othman ordered S. Guna, 33, to serve his sentence from the date of his arrest on July 3. She also ordered him to serve an additional nine months in prison if he fails to pay the fine.
The father of three, who has a disability affecting his hand, was jointly charged with Myanmar national Mohd Sharuf Nizam Mohd Hanif, 26, and another individual who remains at large, for breaking into a house and stealing a gold chain, gold bracelet, gold lockets and a mobile phone.
They allegedly committed the offence at a house in Jalan Bukit Setongkol here at 9.58am on June 25.
The charge, framed under Section 457 of the Penal Code, carries a jail term of between five and 14 years, as well as a fine.
Deputy public prosecutor B. Deevagaran Naidu appeared for the prosecution, while Guna was represented by National Legal Aid Foundation lawyer Rahiza Zulkifli.
In mitigation, Rahiza pleaded for a minimum sentence, saying her client, who holds a disabled person card, was unemployed and received monthly assistance from the Welfare Department.
She said Guna, who is married with children, had saved the court's time by pleading guilty and had repented for his actions.
Deevagaran called for a deterrent sentence, saying it should serve as a lesson to the accused as the victim had suffered losses as a result of the offence.
Ellyna then sentenced Guna to 14 months' imprisonment and fined him RM3,000.
Meanwhile, Sharuf Nizam, a United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) cardholder, claimed trial after being jointly charged with Guna.
Deevagaran proposed bail at RM7,000 with two sureties, but Mohd Shuhairil Ziqrul Sapi, who represented Sharuf Nizam, sought a lower amount, saying his client, who works for a waste disposal company, needed to support his wife and children on his RM1,400 monthly salary.
Ellyna set bail at RM4,500 with one surety and fixed July 29 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested over alleged shooting threat against MCBA officer
Article:
KLANG: Police have arrested a 46-year-old man for allegedly threatening to shoot an assistant environmental health officer from the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA).
South Klang police chief Assistant Commissioner Lim Jit Huey said police received a report on July 14 from the victim, who is attached to Port Klang MCBA.
He said the case is being investigated under Section 506 of the Penal Code for criminal intimidation, which carries a punishment of up to seven years' imprisonment, a fine, or both upon conviction.
"Following the report, officers from the South Klang district Criminal Investigation Department arrested a
46-year-old local man on July 14.
The suspect has no previous criminal record.
"The investigation is ongoing to complete the probe," he said in a statement today.
It was reported that a man believed to be a shipping agent allegedly threatened to shoot an MCBA enforcement officer while the officer was carrying out official duties at Port Klang.
Port Klang MCBA Commander Datuk Nik Ezanee Mohd Faisal said a police report had been lodged at the Klang Selatan district police headquarters following the incident.
"Any form of threat, intimidation, harassment or attempt to obstruct public officers from carrying out their duties is a serious offence and will not be tolerated.
"The police will investigate these allegations," he said in a statement today.
The incident is believed to have
occurred at 2pm on Tuesday.
It was also reported that dissatisfaction over an inspection process is believed to have prompted an individual to threaten to shoot an MCBA officer.
MCBA director-general Datuk Seri Mohd Shuhaily Mohd Zain said preliminary checks found that the incident occurred after the suspect, believed to be an agent, allegedly failed to cooperate with officers during the initial stage of an inspection at Port Klang.
He said the MCBA officer was merely carrying out his duties in accordance with standard operating procedures before a misunderstanding arose with the suspect.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chow: Victoria Specialist Hospital project resumes, targets completion by 2029
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The long-delayed Victoria Specialist Hospital project has resumed, with its original local authority approvals remaining intact and construction expected to restart next month.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the state government was informed during a special meeting on July 8 that the project would proceed without changes to its approved plans following a change in the management of Kuala Lumpur International Dental Centre Sdn Bhd (KLIDC) in 2024.
"The project has resumed without any changes to the building layout or the original approvals granted by the local authority.
"We were also informed that the building plan approval and Commencement of Works (CoW) issued by the Penang Island City Council (MBPP) remain valid," he said.
Chow said the 21-storey mixed development, approved under planning permission granted on March 19, 2019, comprises a specialist hospital, hotel, mechanical parking system and supporting facilities.
He said site clearing, hoarding installation, pile testing, bored piling and earthworks began after approvals were obtained in January 2020, with piling and earthworks completed by October that year despite movement control restrictions during the Covid-19 pandemic.
"Construction was subsequently delayed for a prolonged period due to various constraints, including disruptions to construction material supply chains, labour shortages, rising construction costs, as well as financing and project cash flow challenges," he said.
Chow said the state government welcomed the latest progress, with the appointment of the main contractor now under way and construction expected to resume in mid-August.
Critics recently asked the state government to explain why the proposed development on the Taman Manggis land here had remained undeveloped for such a prolonged period.
They pointed out that the prime parcel of land was originally designated for public and affordable housing projects, raising concerns over years of inactivity and uncertainty surrounding its future.
The Taman Manggis site has long been the subject of public and political scrutiny, with many questioning whether it will eventually fulfil its original purpose of benefiting the local community.
Last month, Chow asked KLIDC to clarify the status and
future
direction of its proposed development on the Taman Manggis land.
Meanwhile, Architectnia architect, Nora Che Idris, said the project was revived in 2024 under KLIDC's new management and would proceed based on the original approved design.
"There are no changes to the approved layout or planning permission. We are proceeding with the original approvals to ensure they remain valid and comply with current requirements," she said.
Nora said the consultant team had reconfirmed the existing approvals with all relevant technical agencies, including MBPP, the Fire and Rescue Department, Health Ministry, Indah Water Konsortium and Tenaga Nasional Bhd.
She said the tender for the main superstructure closed on June 27 after attracting five pre-qualified bidders.
"We are targeting to award the contract in early August, with construction to commence in mid-August," she said.
Nora said construction would take 36 months, with the Certificate of Completion and Compliance expected to be issued by August 2029.
The development comprises a 151-bed multidisciplinary specialist hospital and a 264-room hotel. It is expected to create about 800 jobs, including for healthcare professionals, specialists and support staff.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: ICE suspends traffic stops in wake of Maine fatal shooting
Article:
BIDDEFORD: The US Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency has ordered its officers to suspend most vehicle stops around the country, after agents fatally shot two men six days apart during traffic stops in Texas and Maine, officials said on Tuesday.
The shift in arrest tactics, which President Donald Trump's border czar, Tom Homan, called a "temporary pause" in vehicle stops, was implemented one day after an ICE agent killed a driver from Colombia in the coastal Maine town of Biddeford, about 24 km south of Portland.
It came one week after a similar deadly shooting of a Mexican national by an ICE officer in Houston.
Although both the men shot were characterised by the US Department of Homeland Security, parent agency of ICE, as being "illegal aliens," DHS officials have acknowledged neither was the intended target of deportation operations that led to their demise.
No video footage of either shooting has surfaced, and federal authorities have offered no evidence to support contentions that either man posed a threat to ICE agents or the public at large justifying the use of lethal force to stop them.
The back-to-back incidents brought to at least seven the number of people shot dead during immigration enforcement operations since January 2025, when Trump returned to office and launched a campaign of mass deportations.
Since the beginning of June, ICE arrests in Maine have more than quadrupled to around 70 per day in early July, according to internal ICE data shared with Reuters by a source.
The shooting in Maine sparked immediate protests on Monday, and further demonstrations were taking place on Tuesday.
"It's not a policy change, it's a temporary pause," Homan said of the vehicle-stop suspension in an interview on the Fox News Channel on Tuesday.
"This is going to be a short-term review to make sure ICE agents are safe and doing the right thing." He added that officers had other options for taking people into custody besides stopping them in their vehicles.
DHS asserted in a statement nearly 12 hours after Monday's shooting that an ICE officer "fearing for public safety" opened fire when the driver attempted to flee.
Officials have not explained how the driver might have posed a threat that would justify the shooting.
According to ICE policy, officers may use deadly force only when there is "imminent danger of serious bodily injury or death to the officer or to another person" and is not authorized "solely to prevent the escape of a fleeing suspect."
US Senator Angus King, an independent from Maine who caucuses with the Democrats, told reporters that the agents involved were not wearing body cameras.
DHS said the agents were surveilling the last known address of someone with a final order of removal from the country, and followed a car they saw departing the residence. DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin later told King that the driver of that car — the man killed in the shooting —  was not the target of the operation, according to a spokesperson for the senator.
Immigration advocates said the person shot was a 26-year-old man from Colombia who was authorized to work in the US Colombian President Gustavo Petro in a post on X identified the man killed as Johan Sebastian Duran.
"He was killed because he was believed to be an inferior being with no rights," Petro wrote, adding that he expected Colombia's foreign service in the U.S. to take the "swiftest possible" actions to hold those responsible accountable.
Duran, who grew up in Bucaramanga, Colombia, had a partner and a young daughter and worked two jobs, including food delivery, the Portland Press Herald reported.
Neighbours told the newspaper that his partner and daughter witnessed the aftermath of the shooting and could be heard crying in the street.
One eyewitness, Daniel Boucher, 71, who lives in downtown Biddeford, told Reuters he heard what sounded like firecrackers on Monday morning, rushed to the window and saw a white SUV ram a smaller white car.
Boucher said he then saw an ICE officer emerge from the SUV and pull out the car's driver, who had blood on his face and head.
"I remember hearing the victim say, 'But I tried to stop,'" Boucher said, before the man appeared to stop breathing.
The Maine attorney general's office said it was investigating the shooting alongside local, state and federal authorities.
The shooting came six days after an ICE agent in Houston's heavily Hispanic East End fatally shot a 52-year-old man, Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, after a traffic stop during an immigration enforcement operation.
Salgado was not the target of the operation, a DHS official has said.
ICE said in a statement that Salgado, a Mexican national living in the US illegally for over three decades, rammed a law enforcement vehicle with his van and attempted to run down an officer who fired in self-defence.
The agency offered no evidence to support its account. In similar instances over the past year, initial ICE and DHS statements about the use of force have been contradicted by video footage or other evidence, sometimes in court.
Three men who were riding in Salgado's van have disputed ICE's narrative, according to a lawyer for two of the men.
Arrests of immigrants have surged in recent weeks, even as the Trump administration has moved away from broad sweeps in Democratic-led cities.
Those operations were widely criticised as violent and heavy-handed after two US citizens were killed in January by federal agents in Minnesota.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DAP backs Melaka reps' decision to oppose appointed assemblymen
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The DAP Central Executive Committee (CEC) has backed the decision by its Melaka chapter to oppose the introduction of appointed assemblymen in the state.
DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke said the CEC was briefed on Melaka DAP's position before yesterday's Melaka state assembly sitting.
The assembly passed a constitutional amendment to allow for the appointment of assemblymen.
Melaka DAP's four assemblymen, including the state's sole DAP executive councillor, voted against the bill despite the motion requiring approval by the state legislature.
The four later announced they were
quitting
the Melaka government.
"The CEC unanimously agreed to respect and support Melaka DAP's decision," Loke said in a statement.
Loke said that under the principle of collective responsibility, an executive councillor who cannot support a government motion must relinquish the post.
Voting against the government while remaining a member of the executive council was no longer tenable.
"This is the principle Melaka DAP has acted on," he said.
He added that he had informed Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim that the four assemblymen had submitted their resignation letters, and that their votes against the bill now form part of the official record of the state assembly.
"It is settled, it is inappropriate for the four assemblymen to walk back (on their decision to quit).
"Having taken a stand against the government's motion on the floor of the assembly, resignation was the most principled course of action, in upholding with democratic norms of the Westminster system," he said.
Prime Minister and federal PH chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has asked DAP to defer its exit from the state administration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Rafizi's Bersama to skip Negri polls, focus on Melaka
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Parti Bersama Malaysia will skip the Negri Sembilan election to focus its resources on the upcoming Melaka polls.
Party leaders Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli and Nik Nazmi Nik Ahmad said the Aug 1 Negri Sembilan election would see a fierce contest between Pakatan Harapan and Barisan Nasional.
"The outcome of the polls could influence other issues that have been dragged into the political clash, including the conflict involving the royal household," they said in a statement.
"Taking into account time and resource constraints, we believe it would be more strategic for Bersama to focus on the Melaka election, which is slated to take place in a few months.
"As such, we will not participate in the Negri Sembilan election and instead focus on the Melaka election."
Rafizi and Nik Nazmi said Bersama will meet on Tuesday to identify seats and scrutinise potential candidates so they can be announced in advance.
"We hope that with more time, Bersama can enter the Melaka election better prepared," they said.
In last Saturday's Johor election, all 15 of Bersama's candidates lost their deposits, with none managing to secure more than six per cent of the vote.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Death penalty loom for brothers, lover charged with trafficking MDMA-laced vape liquid
Article:
BATU PAHAT: Two brothers and a woman were charged at the magistrate's court here today with trafficking MDMA-laced vape liquid and possessing ketamine.
The accused, Tong Chia Soon, 35, his younger brother Tong Chia Poh, 30, and Poh's girlfriend, Chloe Ong Jia Xuan, 28, nodded in understanding when the trafficking charge was read before Magistrate Mustaqim Sukarno.
No plea was recorded on the trafficking charge as the offence falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
They were jointly charged with trafficking 1,656 millilitres (ml) of liquid containing methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA), an offence under Section 39B(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, punishable under Section 39B(2) of the same Act, read together with Section 34 of the Penal Code.
If convicted, they face the death penalty or life imprisonment and not fewer than 12 strokes of the cane if the death penalty is not imposed.
The trio also claimed trial to two separate charges of jointly possessing ketamine liquid weighing 1,656ml and 415.40g.
The charges were framed under Section 12(2) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, punishable under Section 39A(1) of the same Act, read together with Section 34 of the Penal Code.
Deputy Public Prosecutor Firdaus Ruslan appeared for the prosecution, while lawyer Suhaila Shafi'uddin represented the accused.
The prosecution applied for a mention date as the chemistry report was not ready. Bail was not offered.
The court allowed the application and fixed Sept 28 for mention pending the chemistry report.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 16 years on, woman continues treatment after acid attack by father
Article:
KUALA BERANG:  Sixteen years after she was doused with acid by her father, Nurul Dahyatul Fazlinda Mat Haizan says the physical and emotional pain has yet to end as she continues undergoing treatment to rebuild her face.
The 24-year-old was only eight when the attack occurred in the early hours of Aug 15, 2010, leaving her with severe facial injuries.
Nurul Dahyatul said she has since undergone five reconstructive surgeries and makes monthly trips to Hospital Kuala Lumpur for laser treatment to soften the scar tissue on her face.
"The most painful procedure involved tissue expansion. Doctors made an opening on the upper part of my forehead and inserted tissue beneath the skin.
"They then injected medication to gradually create a bulge on my forehead, as the skin from that area would later be used in another reconstructive surgery," she said according to Harian Metro.
She said one of the operations lasted 10 hours, during which surgeons also harvested cartilage from the right side of her body to reconstruct her nose.
The surgery was necessary after extensive scarring caused her nasal passages to narrow, making it increasingly difficult for her to breathe.
"The treatment has gone well. Skin from my forehead was grafted onto my nose after the damaged tissue was removed.
"Although the treatment is long and requires regular hospital visits, I am grateful that I can now breathe properly," she said.
Nurul Dahyatul said doctors have scheduled another surgery at the end of the year to reconstruct or replace her nasal bone.
She said the cost of her treatment and travel to Kuala Lumpur has been supported by Seputeh member of parliament Teresa Kok and the Terengganu Islamic Religious and Malay Customs Council, which also assisted in funding the purchase of tissue required for the procedures.
Affectionately known as Lin, she said doctors have informed her that the treatment and reconstruction process will continue for several more years.
"There is still no end in sight, but I have accepted that this long journey is necessary to safeguard my health and prevent further complications if I stop treatment.
"The hospital has become like my second home because I have to return every month for follow-up treatment, in addition to the surgery scheduled for the end of this year," she said.
Despite the trauma she endured, Nurul Dahyatul said she has forgiven her father, who still visits her as they live near each other.
"I see this as a test from God. During the incident, my mother, Che Ku Zaimah Che Ku Awang, 44, and my younger brother, Mohd Irfan Firdaus, 19, were also splashed with acid, but their injuries were not as severe as mine," she said.
Unable to work while undergoing ongoing treatment, Nurul Dahyatul now helps her mother run a food stall in front of their home.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: China turns to electric taxis to soften Hormuz oil shock
Article:
BEIJING: China has an increasingly important buffer against oil price shocks: electric taxis.
Across Chinese cities, taxi usage and ridesharing are booming. In May, people took 3.05 billion trips, with government data showing trips have grown six per cent since the Iran war began at the end of February, versus March to May last year.
The jump reflects a quirk of China's transport structure: fares are falling despite gasoline prices rising.
Analysts say a flood of new drivers searching for work in a sluggish economy combined with cheap electric cars is depressing fares, in turn attracting passengers who want to save on higher petrol costs.
A part-time Beijing ride-hailing driver surnamed Li said fares have fallen 10 to 15 per cent since he started six months ago.
"Competition is intense," the 36-year-old told Reuters at an electric vehicle charging station.
The flip side can be seen on social media. Since gasoline prices began rising in March, hundreds of posts describe how travel by cab or rideshare is cheaper than driving.
"Especially when gas prices are high, I'd rather take a taxi to places that are too far to bike to. That way, I don't have to look for parking or pay for gasoline," said Yang, a 45-year-old owner of a petrol car, who only gave her surname.
With the electrification of taxis, the rideshare boom adds to evidence that transportation in China is becoming less dependent on oil, insulating it against oil shocks such as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
About half of China's 1.3 million-strong taxi fleet is electric, according to the Ministry of Transport, and in major cities it is closer to 100 per cent.
Didi, the main ridesharing app, said it registered another two million hybrid or electric cars last year taking its total non-fossil fuel fleet to eight million cars, with EVs doing 75 per cent of all mileage.
As a result, China burned 10 per cent less gasoline and 14 per cent less diesel in May than a year earlier, even though road freight rose two per cent, and road travel during the May Day long weekend hit an all-time high.
[[nid:1489466]]
Greenpeace forecasts that 90 per cent of taxi and ridesharing mileage will be electric by 2035.
"As fuel prices have gone up, people are driving their own petrol cars less," said Daizong Liu, East Asia director at the Institute for Transportation & Development Policy in China.
"But overall travel demand is still increasing, so more trips are shifting to public transport, such as taxis and the subway."
That flexibility partly explains how China has managed to slash oil imports, which fell 41 per cent in June versus a year ago, without extensively tapping its reserves.
By doing so, China has freed up oil cargoes in a war-constrained global market and helped keep a lid on oil prices.
"The conflict may have accelerated behavioural changes that were already underway, leaving China structurally less dependent on oil than the market has historically assumed," J.P. Morgan analyst Natasha Kaneva said in a note on July 2.
That possibility will be tested as prices for transport fuels in China fall back to pre-war levels.
J.P. Morgan expects gasoline demand to continue dropping in 2027, but at a slower pace than this year, forecasting a year-on-year decline of 50,000 barrels per day compared with this year's decline of 150,000 bpd.
Zhang, 45, an electric car and hybrid owner who only gave her surname, said she usually drives her hybrid in battery mode when fuel prices are high.
"When I saw prices had fallen recently, I went to fill up the tank for my hybrid," she said.
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Keeping cool, Tokyo style: Shorts, sneakers and no ties
Article:
TOKYO: When Tokyo Metropolitan government worker Toru Suda showed up for work in a polo shirt, shorts and sneakers on a sweltering July morning, he was not making a statement.
As Japan braces for another scorching summer, the Tokyo government is encouraging its staff to ditch jackets and ties in favour of shorts, T-shirts and sneakers under the "Tokyo Cool Biz" campaign, aimed at helping staff cope with increasingly severe summer heat and reduce energy costs.
"At first it felt a little strange," said Suda, the 34-year-old public servant in his light blue polo shirt and knee-length navy shorts.
But the sense of awkwardness gradually faded as more colleagues around him started dressing casually, he said at his office in central Tokyo on Tuesday, when temperatures in the capital climbed to around 35C with average humidity of 72 per cent.
Japan is bracing for another hot summer after enduring the hottest summer on record last year, when average nationwide temperatures were 2.36C above average summer, and heatstroke sent more than 100,000 people to hospital.
On Wednesday, the Japan Meteorological Agency and the Ministry of Environment jointly issued heatstroke alerts for the capital, the first this summer, urging people to limit outdoor activities and use air conditioning.
[[nid:1489462]]
Tokyo's "Cool Biz" campaign, which builds on a nationwide initiative first launched in 2005, marks one of the boldest endorsements yet of casual office attire in a country where dark suits and white shirts have long been the norm for office workers, even during the hottest months.
While some customer-facing businesses still expect formal dress, a growing number of employers are giving employees greater discretion for dressing down, particularly when they are not meeting clients.
Retailers such as Aeon have responded by expanding business-casual lines, while apparel makers including Fast Retailing and Aoki are promoting lightweight, stretchable and quick-drying clothing aimed at office workers seeking to balance comfort with a professional look.
For Metropolitan Government workers, the shift is practical rather than symbolic.
"Honestly, I've gotten used to this and it's hard to go back," said Noboru Watanabe, who leads Tokyo's team of climate change countermeasures.
"That said, formal occasions still require formal attire, so I adjust my clothing according to the task and the situation."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trainee teacher charged with sexual assault of 14-year-old student
Article:
IPOH: A trainee teacher pleaded not guilty at the Sessions Court today to six counts of sexual assault against a 14-year-old male student last week.
The accused, Muhammad Mifdzal Mohd Zalani Sham, 25, claimed trial after the charges were read before Judge Ainul Shahrin Mohamad.
The offences were allegedly committed at a house in Kampung Chakadi between 1am and 7.26am on July 5.
The charges under Section 14(a) and Section 14(b) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017 (Act 792) carries a maximum prison sentence of 20 years and rotan upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor M. Raveenaa prosecuted, while the accused was represented by lawyer Ameer Firdaus Mohd Amir.
Raveenaa proposed bail at RM20,000 for each charge with one local surety, along with additional conditions that the accused must not harass the victim or prosecution witnesses and must report to a police station once a month.
However, the lawyer pleaded for a lower bail of RM5,000 for the first three charges and another RM5,000 for the remaining three charges.
He argued that his client, a student at Universiti Pendidikan Sultan Idris (UPSI), was not a high flight risk and had cooperated fully with the authorities.
"The accused is currently awaiting his examination results. He is a trainee teacher and is waiting for an interview call. He appeared in court today without handcuffs as he was released on police bail."
"The accused, who is originally from Penang, is ready to provide full cooperation as he intends to clear his name to pursue his career as a civil servant," he said.
The court allowed bail at RM5,000 for the first set of charges and RM5,000 for the second set with one surety. The court set Aug 20 for mention and for submission of documents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Communications and Multimedia Bill 2026 passed with security safeguards
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Dewan Rakyat today passed the Communications and Multimedia (Amendment) Bill 2026 after Deputy Communications Minister Teo Nie Ching assured lawmakers that its national security provisions were narrowly scoped.
Teo said the legislation introduced a national Universal Service Provision (USP) initiative that incorporated national security considerations while remaining aligned with the objectives of the Communications and Multimedia Act (Act 588) and the existing USP regulatory framework.
"The proposed amendments are not intended to expand the scope of Act 588 to any other agency's jurisdiction," she said while winding up the debate during the second reading of the Bill.
Teo said national security determinations under the national USP initiative would be made by the National Security Council (NSC), ensuring that the minister's powers were exercised in a targeted manner and could not be read as open-ended authority over national security matters.
On concerns that the expanded USP fund scope could shift the financial burden onto end users, Teo said the fund was financed entirely through contributions from licensees under the Act at a prescribed percentage, channelled into a trust fund.
The fund could only be used for the purposes of USP as defined by the Act, covering the installation of network facilities and the provision of network or application services.
"The issue of transferring the USP burden to end users does not arise," she said.
On concerns regarding media freedom, Teo cited a statement by the prime minister on July 7 in the Dewan Rakyat that complaints against journalists would no longer result in automatic enforcement action but would first be referred to the Malaysia Media Council for review.
She said no prosecution cases involving journalists had been recorded in the past year despite various complaints being received.
The Bill was passed on its third reading without amendment.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor BN sends 26 division machinery to boost Negri Sembilan campaign
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Barisan Nasional (BN) Johor will deploy its election machinery from 26 divisions to assist the coalition's campaign in the Negri Sembilan state election on Aug 1.
Johor BN secretary Datuk Abdul Halim Suleiman said the machinery would be mobilised to support BN divisions in the state ahead of nomination day this Saturday.
"Insya-Allah, all machinery will move there soon, possibly tomorrow or the day after.
"BN Johor will bring the spirit of the Gelombang Biru to Negri Sembilan to help secure victories for BN candidates," he told the New Straits Times, today.
Halim said state BN would continue to campaign using the 'cara Johor' (the Johor way) approach by engaging voters in a disciplined and respectful manner.
He was speaking after visiting residents in Kampung Bukit Pulai and Kampung Tanjung Langsat, Pasir Gudang, following complaints over damaged and pothole-ridden roads.
Halim, who is also Tebrau Umno division chief, said the machinery would apply the experience and strategies used during the recent state election to win support in Negri Sembilan.
The Negri Sembilan state assembly, which has 36 seats, was dissolved on June 4 after 14 BN assemblymen withdrew support for Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun.
The Election Commission (EC) has set Aug 1 as polling day, with nomination and early voting scheduled for July 18 and July 28 respectively.
In the Johor election on July 11, Halim won the Tiram seat with a majority of 28,255 votes after securing 49,965 votes.
He defeated Pakatan Harapan's Nor Zulaila Abd Ghani, Perikatan Nasional's Khirul Muntanazar Ismail and Bersama candidate Harith Fakhrudin Abdul Malek.
Johor BN won a landslide victory in the election, forming the state government after capturing 48 of the 56 seats contested, while PH won eight seats.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Global air travel nears 10bil passengers; KLIA breaks into world's top 20
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Global air travel moved closer to the 10 billion-passenger mark in 2025, with the Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) in Sepang emerging as one of the biggest gainers in global rankings, according to data released by Airports Council International (ACI) World.
KLIA climbed six places to 20th from 26th in 2024, the largest jump among the world's 20 busiest airports, after handling 63.4 million passengers, an 11 per cent increase from a year earlier.
The airport's growth was driven by a 17 per cent rise in domestic traffic, an 8.7 per cent increase in international passenger volumes, expanded airline capacity and stronger connectivity with China and other key markets, strengthening its position ahead of Visit Malaysia 2026.
Shanghai Pudong International Airport recorded the second-largest improvement, rising five places to fifth from 10th after passenger traffic grew 10.7 per cent, supported by China's easing of visa policies, expanded international connectivity and a rebound in overseas travel.
The 2026 ACI World Airport Traffic Dataset showed global passenger traffic reached 9.8 billion in 2025, up 3.7 per cent from 2024 and 6.5 per cent above pre-pandemic 2019 levels.
The dataset, covering 2,817 airports across more than 180 countries and territories, showed the world's 20 busiest airports handled 1.59 billion passengers, accounting for 16 per cent of global traffic. Airports in the Asia-Pacific region recorded the biggest shifts in the rankings, driven by infrastructure expansion, improved connectivity and strong tourism demand.
ACI World director general Justin Erbacci said global air travel is approaching a historic milestone, but record demand is also exposing growing pressures on capacity.
"The planning and investment decisions made today will determine whether aviation can meet the demands of the next generation of travellers," he said.
Global air cargo volumes rose 3.3 per cent to more than 131 million metric tonnes in 2025, exceeding 2019 levels by 7.4 per cent, as e-commerce growth and supply chain reconfiguration continued to support demand for air freight.
The world's top 20 cargo airports handled 53.1 million metric tonnes, or nearly 41 per cent of global cargo volumes, up 2.4 per cent from 2024.
Aircraft movements worldwide increased 2.0 per cent to more than 103.1 million in 2025, returning to around pre-pandemic levels. The top 20 airports recorded 11.3 million aircraft movements, representing 11 per cent of the global total, up 2.5 per cent from 2024 and 2.8 per cent above 2019 levels.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang unity government remains intact, says Chow
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang unity government remains intact, with cooperation among its component parties continuing smoothly, state PH chairman Chow Kon Yeow said.
Chow, who is also Penang Chief Minister, said there were no issues affecting the working relationship within the Penang unity government and that no party had proposed changing the existing arrangement.
"So far, there is no issue with us working together. Everything remains status quo.
"At the moment, no party is proposing a new direction. Our colleagues often say the Penang unity government remains intact, and that is true based on the current situation," he told reporters today.
His remarks came after Melaka DAP lawmakers quit the state government yesterday over the appointed assemblymen amendment.
They
are Allex Seah Shoo Chin (Kesidang), Kerk Chee Yee (Ayer Keroh), Low Chee Leong (Kota Laksamana) and Leng Chau Yen (Banda Hilir).
Bukit Katil assemblyman Adly Zahari of Amanah also announced his withdrawal, bringing the total number of PH lawmakers in Melaka moving to the opposition bench to five.
Following the announcement, all five assemblymen resigned from their positions in the state administration.
Meanwhile, commenting on PH's performance in the recent Johor state election, Chow said the coalition accepted the outcome with an open mind and would study the various analyses on the factors behind the results.
"We welcome these views as constructive feedback to help us address weaknesses, strengthen our strengths and refine our strategies so that we can continue to be the choice of voters.
"There are many possible reasons behind the outcome. It is
important
that we continue to earn and maintain the confidence of the people," he said.
Chow said the state PH leadership had already begun preparations for the next general election, with its various sub-committees directed to meet and submit progress reports at the coalition's next meeting in early August.
